title: Generalized multilevel amplitude damping channels and their thermodynamic performances
authors: Vito Vetrano, Vittorio Giovannetti, Vasco Cavina
abstract: We introduce a new class of quantum channels, the Generalized Multilevel Amplitude Damping (GMAD) channels, to model noise and decoherence effects in a qudit coupled to a thermal environment. The degradation of energetic resources under GMADs is investigated by evaluating work functionals and ergotropic capacitances, with particular attention to the coherent and incoherent contributions to ergotropy, for which we introduce new quantifiers. Our analysis sheds light on how to optimally prepare a qudit in a thermal environment in order to preserve its value from the perspective of work extraction, and reveals several counterintuitive phenomena: the ergotropic capacitance of a GMAD channel is not monotonic in the temperature of the environment; moreover, iterating the map can lead to crossings between ergotropic functionals at different temperatures, indicating the presence of a Markovian Mpemba effect.
summary: This paper introduces the Generalized Multilevel Amplitude Damping (GMAD) channel to model qudit dynamics in thermal environments. It reveals that these channels can exhibit counterintuitive thermodynamic behaviors, such as non-monotonic work extraction and a quantum-driven Mpemba effect.
main_problem: Characterizing the thermodynamic performance and energetic capacities of a new class of quantum channels (GMAD) for work extraction.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that GMAD channels exhibit non-monotonic ergotropic capacitance with respect to temperature and can drive a coherent ergotropic Mpemba effect.
method: The study uses Kraus representation derivation, ergotropy analysis (total, coherent, and incoherent), and asymptotic analysis of work functionals (shell-maximal and ball-maximal).
model_or_system: A d-level quantum system (qudit) coupled to a thermal environment at inverse temperature beta, modeled via the Generalized Multilevel Amplitude Damping (GMAD) channel.
key_observables: Ergotropy (total, coherent, and incoherent), work capacitance, and Maximal Asymptotic Work-Energy Ratio (MAWER).
important_parameters: Temperature (beta), input energy (E), coupling constants (g_ij), and the number of channel iterations.
main_assumptions: The system Hamiltonian is assumed to be non-degenerate, and the interaction is energy-preserving.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a qualitative sketch of the GMAD channel on a 4-level system; Figure 2 displays ergotropy as a function of energy for various temperatures; Figure 3 shows local ergotropic capacitance; Figure 6 illustrates the decomposition of unitary evolution.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the GMAD channel, derives its microscopic Kraus representation, analyzes its thermodynamic properties (ergotropy and capacitance), and demonstrates specific phenomena like the Mpemba effect.
why_interesting: It provides new insights into how dissipative dynamics and thermal environments can be exploited for work extraction and reveals purely quantum thermodynamic effects like the coherent Mpemba effect.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Qualitative sketch of the effect of the GMAD chan- nel on a generic 4-level system. Following the definitions in Eq. (1), we can isolate three blocks of Kraus operators (cor- responding to m = 1, 2, 3) acting on the first 2, 3, 4 levels, respectively. The properties of the coefficients u(m) ik appear- ing in the rates in Eq. (2) are made explicit in the bottom of the figure: for a fixed m they can be arranged as entries of an m + 1 dimensional unitary matrix. Jumps connecting the energy levels are depicted using distinct colors and hues, and the entries of the unitary matrices are highlighted using the same colors as the corresponding transitions.

FIG. 2. (From top left to center right) 1-cell shell-maximal work functionals for the channel G with s1 = 0.5, ¯s = 0.99, α0 = 0.99, as a function of the energy E, for different val- ues of β. The dashed line represents the corresponding MAD channel (β = +∞). (Bottom left) 1-cell shell-maximal er- gotropy as a function of E for the channel G with s1 = 0.01, ¯s = 0.99, α0 = 0.745, and different values of β. The non- monotonicity of the maximal ergotropy in the temperature is evident. (Bottom right) 1-cell shell-maximal ergotropy as a function of E for the channel G with s1 = 0.99, ¯s = 0.255, α0 = 0.5, for different values of β. FIG. 3. (Red solid curve) Local ergotropic capacitance for the GMAD channel G with s1 = 0.5, ¯s = 0.745, α0 = 0.745, β = 1, acting on a three-level system with spectrum (0, 0.8, 1). (Cyan solid curve) 1-cell ball-maximal incoherent ergotropy for the same channel; even if it is not concave in e, its con- cave envelope (cyan dot-dashed curve), here indicated as bEi, bounds from below the respective capacitance. (Gray dotted line) Line with slope equal to the MAWER for the same chan- nel; its value is Jloc(G) ≈0.919 .
